title: The Attention Extraction: How India's Digital Dividend Is Becoming a Cognitive Deficit
thesis: India's digital-access success has inverted: the same infrastructure that could have accelerated learning, production, and civic participation is now optimized to capture and monetize attention, producing measurable costs in cognition, mental health, economic output, and social trust—not because users are weak, but because the system is designed to keep them scrolling.
primary_reader: Indian citizens, educators, parents, and builders who sense that screen time has become excessive but have not yet seen the aggregated public evidence.
intended_outcome: Readers can name three concrete, evidence-based costs of the attention economy in India and see that the problem is designed before it is personal.
artifact_fit: An article fits because the reusable value is a sourced, compressed diagnosis that turns scattered public data into a coherent narrative before later articles prescribe solutions.
smallest_viable_version: A single 2,500–3,500 word diagnosis built around eight to ten verified public data points, with no prescriptive advice.
scope_risks: The article could expand into a white paper, drift into prescriptive territory, or overstate causation from correlational data. It must stay compressed, diagnosis-only, and carefully caveated.
alternatives_considered: A data dashboard or annotated source list could supplement the article later, but the first artifact needs a narrative arc and human scenes.
claims: India has near-universal digital access, but entertainment and social media use crowd out education and production among young users.
A large share of digital advertising revenue in India flows to foreign platforms, while local safety, quality signals, and creator sustainability lag behind.
Short-form feeds use variable rewards, infinite scroll, autoplay, and personalized recommendations to extend engagement beyond user intent.
The costs of the attention economy appear across attention spans, productivity, youth mental health, and social trust.

sources.title: India Budget: Economic Survey 2025-26 Education and Health Chapter
PIB: NCERT Mental Health and Well-being Survey of School Students
PMC: The adverse impact of mobile phone short-form video addiction on attentional functions and self-control
PMC: Social media and mental health among Indian adolescents
Milli et al.: Engagement, User Satisfaction, and the Amplification of Divisive Content on Social Media (PNAS Nexus)
Yale News: Likes and shares teach people to express more outrage online
Steelcase: Our 47-Second Attention Span with Gloria Mark (transcript)
Gallup State of the Global Workplace: India Country-Level Data
IBEF: Tech giants generate over US$7.13 billion in advertising revenue in FY24

sanitized_summary: This article diagnoses how India's digital-access success has inverted into an attention-extraction economy. It uses public government, academic, and industry sources to show that entertainment and social media crowd out educational use among young people, advertising revenue concentrates in foreign platforms, feed design extends engagement beyond intent, and costs appear across attention spans, productivity, youth mental health, and social trust. The article stays diagnosis-only, uses historical analogies and life-phase scenes, and reserves solutions for follow-up articles.
